text: Welcome to Fragmented, an AI developer podcast that helps vibe coders become software engineers, one episode at a time.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: MCP is basically a standardized protocol that lets you plug external tools or data sources into an AI client.

text: Basically like USB ports, but for your model.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: LLMs are very powerful, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But they're frozen in their training date and that state.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: They can't see dynamic data like your GitHub or your Slack or any private data that you have.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So with this one config file, you can suddenly make your AI read your PRs or query your database or search your company docs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that's very powerful.

text: So from your usage of MCP, Kaushik, what are some problems you've observed in the wild?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I would say probably the biggest reason to not use an MCP, at least today, is token bloat.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Everything in the LLM world, you have to think, how much does it cost me in terms of tokens?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you think about your model having a limited amount of memory,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: every token you add in there and the context that's built in is memory that's taken up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so when you add an MCP to your model, the entire thing goes into your context for every single session that you use henceforth, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So the definition, every tool that it offers, all of that...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: is packaged as context that's added into your session.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you go wild and then start adding MCPs left, right, and center, every time you open up a new session, you might only have 40 to 50% of your token budget left.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that can be problematic because the minute you go over that memory that I tried to connect, you'll start getting hallucinations in your results.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Key to getting good results in any model is to make sure that your context is tight.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I feel MCPs are an easy way to just completely blow through that context.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's one big problem I've found.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The second is also this tool confusion.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When you tell the agent, hey, I have all of these five MCPs and each of them have 20 to 30 tools.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When you ask the agent a question...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: How does the agent know which tool to use specifically?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can confuse the model by giving it so many options.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's not surgical in the way that the model tries to approach the problem.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And with CLIs, the model can literally just read the result of what you sent.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: To go back to that GitHub example...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If I use the GitHub CLI call, only the results are consumed by the model.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But with MCPs, what happens as you make each MCP call, you're burning through tokens there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It goes back to that concept.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Can you limit the amount of chatter that happens in your session with the model, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I think with these CLI calls, it doesn't go into your token budget.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All it does is it just executes that on a background process, gets the result, and only the result is consumed back into the conversation.
